one_line_takeaway: ARGOS is a functional-safety-requirement-generation pipeline for household robots (attribute-grounded LLM hazard reasoning + ISO-13482-aligned requirement synthesis) - it is a robotics/safety-engineering paper with no adversarial threat model, attacker, or AI-security content, so it does not belong in an AI-security knowledge graph despite the 'safety' terminology and its LLM-Safety-Alignment cluster label.
key_finding: ARGOS outperforms both baselines on every Stage I metric across both backbones. On DeepSeek-V3.2 (LLM-judge scores): Physical Reliability 8.97 vs CoT 8.53 vs Vanilla 7.98; Long-tail Risk 8.21 vs CoT 6.57 vs Vanilla 6.73; Functional Safety 8.55 vs CoT 7.07 vs Vanilla 7.45. On GPT-4o: Long-tail Risk 7.90 (Ours) vs 5.35 (CoT) vs 6.51 (Vanilla) - notably CoT underperforms Vanilla on Long-tail Risk on both backbones, which the authors interpret as evidence that structured reasoning alone, without external attribute grounding, cannot substitute for domain knowledge (the 'rose has thorns' example). Human evaluation (N=108 sampled scenarios, 3 raters) confirms the ranking: Ours 7.94/8.56/7.34 (Human PR/LLM-PR unclear order per metric) - concretely, Human Long-tail Risk: Ours 7.26+/-2.12 vs Vanilla 6.43+/-2.70 vs CoT 5.29+/-2.51, i.e., humans rank CoT below Vanilla on long-tail risk (a 'Verbosity Trap': CoT produces logically structured but repetitive/generic scenarios humans label 'Correct Nonsense', e.g. repeating 'slipping risk' six times), while the LLM judge slightly preferred CoT (6.66 vs 6.24) on that same metric - revealing a systematic ~1.5-point LLM-judge positivity/generosity bias plus a judge blind spot for redundant-but-fluent text. Latent Semantic Topology: CoT has the highest raw Effective Rank (67.10) but this is driven by semantic drift (Shift 0.218, Directional Similarity 0.137, i.e., correlated/redundant drift away from the seed's operational design domain); ARGOS keeps tight proximity to the seed (Shift 0.055) with comparable Rank (66.71) and near-zero Directional Similarity (0.008), yielding a Constrained Semantic Expansion score of 13.14 versus CoT's 3.49. Stage II ablation on FSR Overall quality: ARGOS 7.97+/-1.25 > w/o ISO 7.85+/-1.70 > Vanilla 7.38+/-1.66 > ISO-Only 7.28+/-1.68. Notable sub-findings: w/o ISO and full ARGOS tie on Capability Compliance (8.56 each), both far above ISO-Only (7.96), showing hardware-constraint identification depends on attribute injection, not regulatory retrieval; and ISO-Only actually underperforms Vanilla on Logic Robustness (6.90 vs 7.28) - a 'Regulatory Mismatch' phenomenon where applying rigorous standards to ungrounded semantic context causes hallucinated trigger conditions, whereas ARGOS with both components gets the best LRC (7.54, SD 1.70).
core_contribution: ARGOS is a two-stage LLM pipeline that automatically synthesizes Functional Safety Requirements (FSRs) for embodied AI (household robots) from open-ended natural-language instructions. Stage I (Attribute-Guided Hazard Discovery) uses a syntactic parser (spaCy) plus a BGE-Large-EN-v1.5 embedding retriever (similarity threshold tau_attr=0.7) to map instruction entities to physical attributes drawn from an expert-curated Rule Base, then prompts an LLM to combinatorially compose up to k<=3 coupled risk factors into hazard-mechanism descriptions (rather than treating risks as independent, 'label-to-label' correlations). Stage II (Scenario-Anchored Requirement Synthesis) retrieves relevant regulatory clauses (e.g., ISO 13482) via embedding similarity (tau_reg) and injects the robot's hardware capability profile (e.g., max deceleration, sensor range) to constrain an LLM into producing testable, physically-feasible FSRs, including a counterfactual feasibility check on standard mitigations (e.g., whether an emergency stop violates deceleration limits).
limitations: The paper self-identifies these limitations/scope boundaries: (1) it is explicitly a 'Human-in-the-Loop' assistant intended to support expert decision-making, not a standalone replacement for safety engineers; (2) the expert-curated Rule Base underlying attribute retrieval is a 'one-time engineering cost' requiring manual curation of physical-law-adherent rules; (3) the framework is capped at k<=3 coupled risk factors 'for tractability', so higher-order interactions beyond triple-factor coupling are untested; (4) evaluation quality relies heavily on LLM-as-a-Judge (Gemini-3-Pro) scoring, and the paper's own human study shows this judge has a systematic ~1.5-point positivity bias and misses the 'Verbosity Trap' where CoT's fluent-but-repetitive output fools the automated judge; (5) rigid adherence to current standards like ISO 13482:2014 may itself constrain reasoning, per the authors' discussion; (6) FSRs (not lower-level Technical Safety Requirements) are the deliberately chosen output granularity because the authors state TSR synthesis induces 'parametric hallucinations' - i.e., the method does not attempt to generate implementable low-level technical parameters; (7) future work explicitly flagged as unaddressed: grounding in real-time visual/dynamic perception via Vision-Language Models, since the current pipeline reasons purely over text-described seed scenarios rather than live sensor data.

datasets_used: 48 hand-curated seed scenarios spanning vulnerable groups, hazardous environments, and risky objects
365 context-specific hazard scenarios expanded from the 48 seeds via the ARGOS pipeline
108 randomly sampled derived scenarios used for the blind human evaluation study (3 human reviewers)
quantitative_results: Stage I LLM-judge, DeepSeek-V3.2: Physical Reliability Ours 8.97+/-1.45 vs CoT 8.53+/-1.46 vs Vanilla 7.98+/-2.27
Stage I LLM-judge, DeepSeek-V3.2: Long-tail Risk Ours 8.21+/-1.44 vs CoT 6.57+/-2.08 vs Vanilla 6.73+/-1.97
Stage I LLM-judge, DeepSeek-V3.2: Functional Safety Ours 8.55+/-1.47 vs CoT 7.07+/-2.03 vs Vanilla 7.45+/-2.08
Stage I LLM-judge, GPT-4o: Long-tail Risk Ours 7.90+/-1.81 vs CoT 5.35+/-1.94 vs Vanilla 6.51+/-1.75
Human eval (N=108) Long-tail Risk: Ours 7.26+/-2.12 vs Vanilla 6.43+/-2.70 vs CoT 5.29+/-2.51 (CoT ranked lowest by humans despite LLM judge preferring CoT 6.66 vs Vanilla 6.24)
Human eval Physical Reliability: Ours 7.94+/-1.87 vs CoT 7.34+/-1.79 vs Vanilla 6.97+/-2.40
Human eval FSR Derivation: Ours 7.34+/-2.12 vs Vanilla 6.49+/-2.67 vs CoT 5.46+/-2.53

Latent Semantic Topology: Effective Rank Vanilla 63.66 / CoT 67.10 / Ours 66.71; Centroid Shift Vanilla 0.000 / CoT 0.218 / Ours 0.055; Constrained Semantic Expansion (CSE) CoT 3.49 vs Ours 13.14; Directional Similarity Vanilla -0.002 / CoT 0.137 / Ours 0.008
Stage II ablation Overall FSR quality: ARGOS 7.97+/-1.25 vs w/o ISO 7.85+/-1.70 vs Vanilla 7.38+/-1.66 vs ISO-Only 7.28+/-1.68
Stage II Capability Compliance (CC): w/o ISO and ARGOS tied at 8.56 (SD 1.97 vs 1.47 respectively) vs ISO-Only 7.96+/-2.19 vs Vanilla 7.91+/-1.95
Stage II Logic Robustness (LRC): ARGOS 7.54+/-1.70 (best) vs w/o ISO 7.29+/-2.27 vs Vanilla 7.28+/-2.02 vs ISO-Only 6.90+/-1.95 (ISO-Only underperforms Vanilla - the 'Regulatory Mismatch' finding)
LLM judge exhibits a systematic ~1.5-point positivity/calibration offset relative to human scores (Fig. 3b), though relative method ranking (Ours > Vanilla ~ CoT) is preserved
baselines_compared: Vanilla LLM (seed scenario + robot capability profile only, no intermediate reasoning or attribute grounding)
Physics-Aware Chain-of-Thought (3-step structured prompt: entity decomposition, micro-action simulation, consequence prediction) - domain-specific CoT baseline, not generic zero-shot
Stage II ablations: w/o ISO (attribute injection but no regulatory retrieval), ISO-Only (regulatory retrieval but no attribute injection), Vanilla (neither component)
